title: Fresh Fruit and Fresh Spanish

story.scene: Carlos is already waiting at the market. He promised his mother he would bring a few ingredients for lunch and asks you to help choose them.
story.mission: Today you’ll help Carlos buy ingredients for lunch.
story.continuity: The lesson opens at the exact day, time, and place arranged in Lesson 4. Carlos now reveals the special thing he needed: your help choosing something for the family table.

emotionalArc.startsWith: The learner arrives curious about Carlos’s mysterious market purchase.
emotionalArc.endsWith: The learner feels that Madrid is alive and that they helped a friend inside it.
emotionalArc.earnedBy: Choosing a product, speaking with a vendor, and completing a real purchase for family lunch.

byChoice.apples: My mother loved the apples. The fruit salad disappeared quickly! 🍎
byChoice.bread: The bread was perfect for lunch. Thanks again for helping me choose it! 🥖
byChoice.cheese: That cheese from La Mancha was a great choice. Everyone asked for more. 🧀
byChoice.coffee: The coffee smelled even better at home. Tomorrow, I’ll show you how to order mine. ☕
byChoice.flowers: My mother loved the flowers. She put them right in the middle of the table. 🌹
byChoice.tomatoes: The tomatoes were perfect for lunch. You chose well today! 🍅

microCliffhanger.speaker: Carlos
microCliffhanger.spanish: Perfecto. Ahora que tenemos todo, conozco una cafetería aquí al lado. Preparan mi café con leche favorito. Mañana te enseño a pedir como un local.
microCliffhanger.english: Perfect. Now that we have everything, I know a café just beside here. They make my favorite café con leche. Tomorrow I’ll teach you to order like a local.

carlosIntroduction.title: Help me shop for lunch
carlosIntroduction.message: ¡Hola! Me alegra verte. Before we explore, I promised my mother I’d buy a few things for lunch. Will you help me? You’ll choose what we buy first, point it out in Spanish, ask the price, and thank the vendor.
carlosIntroduction.prompt: Start by telling me: Sí, claro.

canDo: Choose something to buy instead of following a fixed shopping list.
Point out one or more products with este, esta, estos, or estas.
Ask what something costs and understand a simple price.
Ask for an item, choose an amount, and thank the vendor.

recycledLanguage.use: Greet Carlos and the vendor naturally.
Understand who the shopping is for.
Arrive for the plan and make choices together.

objectives: Use demonstratives while pointing at market products.
Complete one short exchange with a vendor.
Personalize the lesson by choosing a product.

essentialPhrases.usage: Carlos uses this to give the learner control of the shopping route.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: What should we buy first, bread or cheese?

essentialPhrases.usage: Point to one product and ask for it.
Point to more than one item.
Learn this as one useful price question for a single item.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: How much does this cheese cost?

essentialPhrases.usage: Say what you need to buy.
Choose a simple amount without learning a full quantity system.
Tell the vendor your order is complete.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Una bolsa, por favor. Nada más, gracias.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: One bag, please. Nothing else, thank you.

essentialPhrases.usage: React naturally after seeing or trying food.

vocabulary.tip: Use esta manzana for this apple.
In some regions you may also hear banana; Carlos teaches plátano here.
Use esta with the feminine noun naranja.
Use estas fresas when pointing to several.
This item is commonly used in the plural.
Use estos tomates when choosing several.
Use este because pan is masculine and singular.
Cruasán is the adapted Spanish spelling.
Use este queso for this cheese.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: How much does this cheese cost?

vocabulary.tip: Leche is feminine: esta leche.
The written accent marks the final stress.
Zumo is common in Spain; jugo is common in much of Latin America.
Agua is feminine but uses el in singular; treat el agua as one chunk for now.
Use it when talking about what something costs.
Ask for una bolsa after buying something.
Carlos carries the money; your mission is the conversation.
Compramos means we buy or we’re buying in the lesson phrase.
The vendor sells; the customer buys.
You arranged this market visit in Lesson 4.
A market has different puestos for different foods.
Choose the form that matches the person.
These are the things Carlos needs for lunch.
Flowers are one of the learner’s six shopping choices.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Estas flores son para mi madre.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: These flowers are for my mother.

vocabulary.tip: Match the ending to the product: pan fresco, fruta fresca.
Dulce works with masculine and feminine nouns.
Match rico or rica to the noun you describe.
Grande works with masculine and feminine nouns.
Match the ending to the noun.

grammar.topic: Pointing to products: este, esta, estos, estas
grammar.meaning: Say this or these while pointing at something you want.
grammar.explanation: Use este with one masculine item and esta with one feminine item. For more than one, use estos with masculine or mixed items and estas with feminine items. Keep the pointing word directly before the product.

notes.title: One masculine item
notes.text: este pan, este queso, este café

notes.title: One feminine item
notes.text: esta manzana, esta naranja, esta bolsa

notes.title: More than one
notes.text: estos tomates, estos plátanos; estas manzanas, estas flores

notes.title: Point first, explain later
notes.text: At a market, Quiero este... plus the product is enough to begin a useful request.

examples.spanish: Estas flores son para mi madre.
examples.english: These flowers are for my mother.

grammar.practicePrompt: Point to one or more real objects and describe them with este, esta, estos, or estas.

learnerChoices.objective: Choose a product, point it out, ask the price, and complete the purchase.

options.label: Apples
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. Mi madre hace una ensalada de fruta muy rica.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. My mother makes a very tasty fruit salad.
options.memoryCallback: You picked apples first. I’ll remember that.

options.label: Bread
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. Esta panadería siempre huele increíble.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. This bakery always smells incredible.
options.memoryCallback: You went straight for the fresh bread. I’ll remember that.

options.label: Cheese
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. Este queso es de La Mancha y está muy rico.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. This cheese is from La Mancha and is very tasty.
options.memoryCallback: You picked the La Mancha cheese first. I’ll remember that.
options.modelOrder: Quiero este queso pequeño, por favor.

options.label: Coffee
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. El café huele fantástico hoy.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. The coffee smells fantastic today.
options.memoryCallback: Coffee first—I like your style. I’ll remember that.

options.label: Flowers
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. A mi madre le encantan las flores frescas. Siempre pone flores en la mesa cuando tenemos invitados.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. My mother loves fresh flowers. She always puts flowers on the table when we have guests.
options.memoryCallback: You chose flowers for the family table. I’ll remember that.

options.label: Tomatoes
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. Necesitamos tomates para el almuerzo.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. We need tomatoes for lunch.
options.memoryCallback: You picked the tomatoes for lunch. I’ll remember that.

learnerChoices.rejoin: The vendor gives a simple price. The learner confirms the purchase, asks for a bag if needed, and says gracias. Carlos then reveals the nearby café.

dialogue.title: Shopping with Carlos
dialogue.setting: Carlos meets you at the market entrance and lets you choose the first stall.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Hi! I’m glad to see you.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Prometí comprar ingredientes para el almuerzo. ¿Me ayudas?
lines.english: I promised to buy ingredients for lunch. Will you help me?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.intent: Choose apples, bread, cheese, coffee, flowers, or tomatoes.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Buena elección. Vamos a este puesto.
lines.english: Good choice. Let’s go to this stall.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.english: Good morning. What do you need?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Perfecto. Una bolsa, por favor. Gracias.
lines.english: Perfect. One bag, please. Thank you.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: ¡Muy bien! Ya tenemos lo primero para el almuerzo.
lines.english: Very good! We already have the first thing for lunch.

options: He needs ingredients for family lunch
He is opening a market stall
comprehension.answer: He needs ingredients for family lunch

comprehension.prompt: Who chooses what to buy first?

options: The learner accepts the price and thanks the vendor
The vendor asks about the learner’s family
comprehension.answer: The learner accepts the price and thanks the vendor

listening.title: Spanish all around you
listening.instructions: Listen, follow along, then check what you understood.
soundscape: bags and café cups in the background
listening.naturalScript: Buenos días. Tenemos pan fresco. — Mamá, quiero este zumo. — ¿Cuánto cuestan estas manzanas? — Tres euros. — Gracias.
listening.slowScript: Buenos días. / Tenemos pan fresco. / Mamá, / quiero este zumo. / ¿Cuánto cuestan / estas manzanas? / Tres euros. / Gracias.

transcript.speaker: Vendor
transcript.english: Good morning. We have fresh bread.

transcript.speaker: Customer
transcript.english: How much do these apples cost?

comprehension.explanation: Tenemos pan fresco means we have fresh bread.
Quiero este zumo means I want this juice.

comprehension.prompt: How much do the apples cost?
What does the customer request after accepting the price?
comprehension.explanation: Una bolsa, por favor asks for one bag, please.

options: How much do these apples cost?
items.answer: How much do these apples cost?

listeningPhrases.note: A vendor may ask this after greeting you.
Listen for este directly before pan.
The number gives you the price.

pronunciation.focus: Mission phrases at a clear market pace
tips: Keep quiero short and smooth: KYE-ro.
In cuánto, clearly pronounce the opening kw sound.
Keep esta separate from está; the lesson pointing word has no written accent.
Let gracias sound warm rather than rushed.

items.note: Blend the first two sounds smoothly.
Keep esta unstressed and connect it to manzana.
Use a relaxed two-syllable rhythm in much of Spain.

speaking.instructions: Carlos guides you through the market purchase one response at a time.

items.label: Listen
items.cue: Hear the complete request once, then order the same item.

items.label: Your turn
items.cue: Ask the price as one familiar chunk.
items.prompt: Ask how much the item costs.
items.focus: Keep the question lively and clear.

items.label: Keep talking
items.cue: Point to several apples and order them politely.
items.focus: Use estas because manzanas is feminine plural.

items.label: One more challenge
items.cue: Tell the vendor you are finished and thank them.

items.label: Finish the conversation
items.cue: Name one or two products you really buy.
items.prompt: Tell Carlos what you normally buy.
items.meaning: I normally buy bread and coffee.
items.focus: Replace pan and café with products true for you.

miniConversation.setting: You are speaking directly with a market vendor while Carlos stands beside you.
miniConversation.goal: Say what you need, choose one or more, ask the price, and close politely.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: Buenos días. Vamos a comprar el almuerzo juntos.
turns.english: Good morning. Let’s buy lunch together.

turns.speaker: Vendor
turns.english: Good morning. What do you need?

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: ¡Excelente! Tú elegiste y compraste todo en español.
turns.english: Excellent! You chose and bought everything in Spanish.

culture.title: A market is a conversation
culture.text: At a busy Spanish market, begin with buenos días or buenas tardes before asking for something. Pointing and saying este or esta is completely useful when you do not know every product name. Some vendors may offer a recommendation or comment on what is freshest; listen for the key product and price, then use gracias to close warmly.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: Los sábados hay mucha gente. El mercado parece una pequeña ciudad.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: There are lots of people on Saturdays. The market feels like a small city.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: Volveremos otro día. Siempre hay algo nuevo que probar.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: We’ll come back another day. There’s always something new to try.

livingWorldInteractions.title: The summer fruit stand
livingWorldInteractions.prompt: The peaches near the entrance smell incredible. Take a closer look.
livingWorldInteractions.carlosSpanish: Los melocotones de este puesto son increíbles en verano.
livingWorldInteractions.carlosEnglish: The peaches at this stand are amazing in summer.

nativeSpeech.note: Common at counters in Spain. Produce ¿Qué necesitas? for now, but recognize this natural version.
Answer nada más, gracias when your order is complete.

commonMistakes.why: Pan is masculine singular, so use este.
Manzana is feminine singular, so use esta.
Tomates is masculine plural, so use estos.
Use the price question as one complete chunk without es.

canDo: I can point out a product with este, esta, estos, or estas.
I can ask a simple price.
I can make and close a short market request.
I can choose what to buy and receive a relevant response from Carlos.
review.conversationReturn: The next time Carlos asks what you want, choose rather than waiting for him to decide.

quiz.prompt: Carlos points to one loaf of bread. Which phrase fits?
quiz.explanation: Pan is masculine singular, so use este.

quiz.prompt: You point to one apple. Choose the correct phrase.
quiz.explanation: Manzana is feminine singular, so use esta.
Use this complete chunk to ask the price of one item.

quiz.prompt: Which product comes from the bakery group?
quiz.explanation: Pan means bread and belongs at a bakery stall.

quiz.prompt: What does fresco mean when describing food?
quiz.explanation: Fresco or fresca describes food that is fresh.
Flores is feminine plural, so use estas.
Tomates is masculine plural, so use estos.

quiz.prompt: Carlos asks ¿Qué compramos primero? Choose a natural answer.
quiz.explanation: Carlos asks what to buy, so point out a product.

quiz.prompt: The vendor asks ¿Qué necesitas? What is the best response?
quiz.explanation: Necesito + product directly answers what you need.

quiz.prompt: The vendor asks ¿Algo más? You are finished. What do you say?
quiz.explanation: This politely says you need nothing else and closes the order.

quiz.prompt: Translate: Estas uvas están muy ricas.
quiz.explanation: Estas points to feminine plural uvas, and muy ricas means very tasty.

quiz.prompt: You chose flowers. Which Carlos response connects the choice to his family?
options: Mi madre siempre pone flores en la mesa.
El mercado abre a las diez.
quiz.answer: Mi madre siempre pone flores en la mesa.
quiz.explanation: This specifically acknowledges the flowers and adds a family detail.

quiz.prompt: Choose the complete, natural market request.
options: Buenos días. Quiero este pan, por favor. ¿Cuánto cuesta?
quiz.answer: Buenos días. Quiero este pan, por favor. ¿Cuánto cuesta?
quiz.explanation: It greets the vendor, identifies the product, asks politely, and requests the price.

quiz.prompt: The vendor says Cuesta tres euros. What should happen next?
options: Accept or decline, then thank the vendor.
Introduce every member of your family.
Ask which day comes after Friday.
quiz.answer: Accept or decline, then thank the vendor.
quiz.explanation: The price answer moves the purchase toward a decision and polite closing.

quiz.prompt: Which sequence completes the purchase naturally?
options: Greet → choose → ask price → accept → thank
Thank → introduce yourself → choose a day
Choose → discuss family → ask the time
quiz.answer: Greet → choose → ask price → accept → thank
quiz.explanation: This sequence follows a real short market interaction.

quiz.prompt: You normally buy coffee and bread. How can you personalize Carlos’s question?
options: Café y pan son las diez.
quiz.explanation: This gives a personal answer using products from the lesson.

quiz.prompt: Carlos says Esta panadería siempre huele increíble after your choice. What did you most likely select?
quiz.explanation: Panadería means bakery, so the comment responds specifically to choosing bread.

quiz.prompt: Carlos lets you lead. Which response shows the full mission?
options: Estas manzanas. Quiero estas manzanas, por favor. ¿Cuánto cuestan? Perfecto, gracias.
Soy de Madrid. Vivo en el mercado.
quiz.answer: Estas manzanas. Quiero estas manzanas, por favor. ¿Cuánto cuestan? Perfecto, gracias.
quiz.explanation: The learner chooses, points out the product, orders it, asks the price, and closes politely.

flashcards.selectionRule: Only encountered Tier 1 products and mission phrases appear. The learner’s selected product is shown first.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Choose the first product you want Carlos to buy.
Tell Carlos what you normally buy.

realLifeMission.title: Carlos Challenge: Help With Lunch
realLifeMission.mission: Lead one complete market purchase for Carlos’s family lunch. You decide what to buy; Carlos does not decide for you.
successCriteria: Choose one of the available products.
Point it out with este, esta, estos, or estas.
Thank the vendor and close naturally.
realLifeMission.completionResponse: ¡Lo hiciste! You didn’t just learn a product—you helped me shop for my family in a real Madrid market.
realLifeMission.correctionRule: Celebrate the successful purchase first. Correct only one demonstrative or request error that affects which product the vendor understands.
realLifeMission.handoff: Perfecto. Ahora que tenemos todo, conozco una cafetería aquí al lado. Preparan mi café con leche favorito. Mañana te enseño a pedir como un local.

passportStamp.title: Market Explorer
passportStamp.description: You chose a product and helped Carlos complete a market purchase for family lunch.
passportStamp.unlockCondition: Complete the Carlos Challenge for Lesson 5.

achievement.title: Helpful Shopper
achievement.description: Help Carlos buy something for his family entirely in Spanish.

carlosClosing: You chose what to buy and completed a real market purchase in Spanish. Thanks for helping with lunch—next, I’ll take you to my favorite café and show you how to order like a local.
